main_summary.summary: Phil Hutchings, former chair of the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee (SNCC), recounts his path from a Cleveland childhood in a politically engaged Black family through Howard University, the Nonviolent Action Group (NAG), Mississippi Freedom Summer, Newark community organizing, and the SNCC chairmanship. Interviewed September 1, 2011, in Oakland, California, at the Black Alliance for Just Immigration offices by Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program. Hutchings describes his parents' Great Migration arrival to Cleveland, his mother's election as Cleveland's first Black Civil Service Commission chief referee under Mayor Carl B. Stokes, and the influence of Robert F. Kennedy campaign work after the 1964 Atlantic City MFDP defeat. He details his work with Tom Hayden in SDS's Economic Research and Action Project (ERAP), the Newark Community Union Project (NCUP), the 1967 Newark Rebellion, the First National Conference on Black Power organized with Nathan Wright Jr., his SNCC chairmanship beginning 1968, the failed SNCC-Black Panther merger, COINTELPRO operations, and his subsequent organizing of the Venceremos Brigades to Cuba. He closes with reflections connecting 1960s civil rights logic to contemporary immigration organizing.
key_themes: Newark Community Union Project and Black political power
Transition from civil rights to Black Power
main_summary.historical_significance:
Hutchings provides primary-source testimony from an organizer who bridged SNCC, SDS, and Black Power formations during the movement's most volatile years (1964-1970). His firsthand accounts of the Newark Rebellion from inside the Central Ward, the SNCC-Panther merger negotiations during his chairmanship, the Venceremos Brigade founding, and Howard University's Nonviolent Action Group fill gaps in the documentary record about Northern Black radical organizing.

The interview captures specific operational details rarely preserved elsewhere: the strategic decision during the Newark Rebellion to target only white-owned property, the political logic behind the 1970 Kenneth A. Gibson mayoral coalition, the internal SNCC deliberations over the Panther program at Huey Newton's February 1968 birthday rally, and the Vietnamese delegation's role in redirecting U.S. radicals toward Cuba work. Hutchings's later trajectory into immigration organizing also documents the conceptual continuity that veterans of the 1960s movement carried into 21st-century globalization-era struggles.

chapters.title: Family Migration to Cleveland
chapters.summary: Hutchings describes his parents' migration to Cleveland—his father from Macon, Georgia, as part of the Northward Migrations, working as a streetcar conductor and later postal clerk; his mother from outside Memphis, Tennessee. He outlines the family's lower-middle-class status, his father's Hatch Act restrictions as a federal employee channeling political interest through the St. James Forum, and meeting Senator Robert A. Taft at age eight in 1952.

chapters.title: Civic Engagement in Cleveland Youth
chapters.summary: Hutchings recounts his mother's run for state representative endorsed by the Cleveland Press, her later appointment as chief referee of the Civil Service Commission under Carl B. Stokes, his paper-route campaign work, and his participation in the Archegos Club, the Council on Human Relations Youth Council, and the World Council on Human Affairs at East High School. He reflects on Cleveland's geography of East Side Black neighborhoods and Shaker Heights school integration.

chapters.title: NAACP Youth Council and Dime-Store Pickets
chapters.summary: Hutchings describes the spring 1960 dime-store informational pickets at Woolworth, Kresge, and Nizernos along Euclid Avenue in solidarity with Southern sit-ins, and the NAACP's dissolution of his youth chapter in response. He explains how the NAACP leadership saw itself as lawyers and viewed direct action as threatening sweetheart deals with Cleveland's white power elite, then introduces the Circle Pines Club connection and the Fair Play for Cuba Committee his mother warned him against.

chapters.title: Howard University and the NAG
chapters.summary: Hutchings describes his mother's last-minute decision to redirect him from Ohio State to Howard University, the foreign-student presence from the Caribbean, Africa, and Iran, and Kennedy's post-debate campus visit. He details the Nonviolent Action Group (NAG)—Stokely Carmichael, Courtland Cox, Ed Brown, Tom Kahn, Bayard Rustin's mentorship—and Project Awareness, which brought Malcolm X and Herbert Aptheker to campus.

chapters.title: First Arrest at the White Rice Inn
chapters.summary: Hutchings describes the spring 1961 sit-in at a Chinese restaurant in Baltimore under Maryland's public accommodations law, working with CORE under Julius Hobson. After initially leaving when the owner refused to call police, the group returned and sat on the steps until arrested. Hutchings characterizes the experience as a personal liberation—making a stand that placed movement work above family and career expectations.

chapters.title: Howard Faculty and Gender in NAG
chapters.summary: Hutchings characterizes Howard as a 13th-grade colonial institution, with most faculty cowed by federal funding and the McCarthy aftermath. He names Robert Martin and a French resistance veteran named Paul as exceptions. He then addresses gender in NAG, naming Jean Bell, Bill Mahoney, Jean Wheeler, Frank Smith, and Ruth Howard as co-organizers, and explains how the group's refusal of campus recognition strengthened cross-school organizing in DC.

chapters.title: Mississippi Summer with MFDP
chapters.summary: Hutchings describes his 1964 work in the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party challenge under the Second Congressional District—Ruleville, where Fannie Lou Hamer organized—gathering voter-registration signatures to take out of state for the Atlantic City challenge. He observes Ivanhoe Donaldson and others doing voter work and reflects on the organizing discipline of speaking with people on their own terms without dumbing down ideas, and Hamer's use of song to capture room sentiment.

chapters.title: Fear, Faith, and Mississippi as Battleground
chapters.summary: Hutchings rejects framing himself as a person of faith, then describes Mississippi as a war zone comparable to Vietnam veterans' descriptions—a pressure cooker where snipers and state officials made daily life precarious. He describes communal project houses, singing as collective courage, and being tear-gassed at the 1964 Cambridge, Maryland confrontation against George Wallace with Reggie Robinson and H. Rap Brown.
related_events: Bruce Berry/Veterans of the Civil Rights Movement

chapters.title: Atlantic City Aftermath and Robert F. Kennedy
chapters.summary: Hutchings describes the depression that settled on the movement after the 1964 MFDP defeat at Atlantic City, and his post-summer work in the Robert F. Kennedy Senate campaign as research and Speaker's Bureau staff. He rationalizes the work as believing Kennedy understood the movement experience and could grow politically beyond his then-current position.

chapters.title: Newark and the SDS/SNCC Bridge
chapters.summary: Hutchings describes how Tom Hayden recruited him at a party in December 1964 to SDS's Economic Research and Action Project (ERAP) in Newark as part of a planned statewide New Jersey movement. He explains being in both SNCC and SDS simultaneously, his 1964 visit to the SDS Ann Arbor conference where he met Richard Flacks, Al Haber, and David Strauss, and his 1965 organizing of the first major SDS anti-Vietnam War march in Washington with Paul Booth.

chapters.title: NCUP, Poverty Program, Central Ward Power
chapters.summary: Hutchings describes the Newark Community Union Project's (NCUP) shift from unemployment to housing and police-brutality organizing, the break with the Clinton Hill Neighborhood Association, and the strategic decision to take over the District 3 area board of the federal poverty program—hiring Jesse Allen as paid organizer with Bessie Smith as chairperson. He recounts the tradeoff of office-based work that drew organizers away from doorstep community contact, and his nomination as community action director defeated by United Community Corporation leadership.

chapters.title: Newark Allies and Black Power Turn
chapters.summary: Hutchings describes confrontations with Newark Democratic Party operatives including Eulis 'Honey' Ward, the alliance with white funder Derek Winans and his community newspaper The Newark Advance, Star-Ledger reporter coverage, Donald Malafronte as Mayor Hugh Addonizio's chief of staff cast as 'arch bad guy,' and Stokely Carmichael's 1966 visit to recruit him to SNCC's Black Power direction. Hutchings shifted NCUP's Central Ward framing from interracial to Black-power-explicit organizing.

chapters.title: Reformist vs. Radical and Press Strategy
chapters.summary: Hutchings articulates the political theory that the Democratic Party functioned as a Northern equivalent of Southern sharecropping—a bonded system catching Black initiative—and explains the United Freedom Party third-line strategy with former Newark Assemblyman George Richardson. He describes SNCC's dependence on white mainstream press to reach Black audiences for lack of independent Black media, and Carmichael's frequent misquotation in that ecosystem.

chapters.title: Inside the Newark Rebellion
chapters.summary: Hutchings recounts being at the corner of 17th Avenue and Springfield Avenue when the July 1967 Newark Rebellion ignited at the police station holding taxi driver John Smith. He describes deciding with neighborhood figure BJ to channel crowd action only against white-owned property, being beaten by police with rubber truncheons alongside bartender Joe Whitley near the Hayes Homes after shouting 'Black Power,' and using civil rights nonviolent training to protect his head from blows.

chapters.title: First National Conference on Black Power
chapters.summary: Hutchings describes the political logic of moving from community to urban to national Black power, working with Nathan Wright Jr. to organize the First National Conference on Black Power after the Newark Rebellion, with LeRoi Jones bringing modern Black nationalism focused on Newark as the battleground. He frames the 1970 Kenneth A. Gibson Black and Puerto Rican mayoral coalition with Hilda Hidalgo, which won when four Italian candidates split the white vote.
keywords: First National Conference on Black Power
related_events: 1967 First National Conference on Black Power Newark

chapters.title: SNCC Chairmanship and Israel
chapters.summary: Hutchings describes SNCC's financial and political costs from anti-Vietnam War positioning and the 1966 Jack Minnis research paper criticizing Zionism, which alienated liberal allies. He recounts being mobbed in New York City when carrying the SNCC newspaper with the Israel article. He frames the liberalism/radicalism rupture around Vietnam and Israel that preceded his chairmanship.
related_events: Martin Luther King Riverside Church April 1967

chapters.title: COINTELPRO and the Panther Merger
chapters.summary: Hutchings describes COINTELPRO operations including the FBI dropping $100 on New Jersey SNCC organizer Irving Davis's bed for information, and the contested SNCC-Black Panther merger negotiated by Eldridge Cleaver from jail, with Carmichael as Prime Minister, James Forman as Foreign Minister, and H. Rap Brown as Minister of Justice announced at Huey Newton's February 17 1968 Oakland birthday rally. Hutchings, then chair, voted that the Panther program was good but not SNCC's, ending the merger as physical tensions rose.
related_events: February 17 1968 Huey Newton birthday rally Oakland

chapters.title: Black Liberators and SNCC's Decline
chapters.summary: Hutchings describes spending time in 1968 with the Black Liberators in St. Louis, getting arrested with them and acquitted after William Kunstler and Hutchings's lawyer-uncle played his speech to the jury (police used an old anti-labor 'unlawful assembly' statute unused since the 1930s). He reflects on SNCC's downward trajectory since Atlantic City, his strategy of a grassroots national network bridging SNCC with local groups like Black Liberators, JOMO in Florida, and the post-Watts LA formations.

chapters.title: Cuba and the Venceremos Brigade
chapters.summary: Hutchings describes the Venceremos Brigade origin: the Vietnamese delegation redirected U.S. radicals from fighting in Vietnam to working in Cuba. SNCC, SDS, the Chicano movement and others organized the first brigade of 200 mostly-white participants and a second brigade of over 800 in March 1970. He recounts staying at the Habana Libre hotel, meeting Ernesto from Angola at Isla de la Juventud, conversations with Vietnamese organizers, and Cuban discipline that recalibrated 'CP time.'

chapters.title: Immigration and Continuing Struggle
chapters.summary: Hutchings closes with reflections on his current organizing at the Black Alliance for Just Immigration, contrasting 1960s logic—which sought federal intervention against segregationist state officials—with immigration work that confronts the federal government as the architect of NAFTA and global economic dislocation. He frames immigration as integrating race, class, and increasingly gender into the analytic frame begun in the 1960s.
